mappings.rationaleAI: The history of magnetism from lodestones through Gilbert, Oersted, Faraday, and Maxwell, Earth's magnetic field and reversals, and magnetic materials and domains comprehensively address magnetism (C) and magnetic materials (C.5).
Faraday's electromagnetic induction (B.3.a/C.4.a), laws of electrolysis, diamagnetism, the Faraday effect, and his concept of field lines as precursor to field theory (D) are directly relevant.

Maxwell's unification of electricity, magnetism, and light through Maxwell's equations, his prediction of electromagnetic waves, and his formulation of field theory directly address electromagnetic theory (E.1.b), the electromagnetic spectrum (E.1.c), Maxwell's equations (E.3.a), and field theory (D).

The early history of electricity from static electricity through the Leyden jar, Franklin's experiments, Galvani, Volta's battery, and Faraday's induction comprehensively addresses electrostatics (A) and electric currents (B).

Tesla's development of alternating current systems, the polyphase AC motor, and the Tesla coil directly address alternating electric currents (B.3) and principles of AC power generation and transmission (B.3.k).

The photon as quantum of electromagnetic radiation, the photoelectric effect, wave-particle duality, and QED directly address the quantum theory of radiation (E.2) including particle nature evidence (E.2.a) and wave-particle duality (E.2.b).
The electromagnetic spectrum from radio waves through gamma rays, Maxwell's theory, and wave-particle duality address the classical theory of radiation (E.1) and the electromagnetic spectrum (E.1.c).
The electron's role in electricity, vacuum tubes, semiconductors, and photoelectric emission addresses the conduction of electricity (B.2) and electron emission (B.2.d).
The physics of electrical conduction in conductors and semiconductors, band theory, and p-n junctions directly address mechanisms of conduction in metals and semiconductors (B.2.b).
Superconductivity as zero electrical resistance, the Meissner effect, and Cooper pairs address conduction mechanisms (B.2.b) and ferromagnetism/magnetic behaviour (C.5.c).

The history of optics from ancient theories through Ibn al-Haytham, Newton's prism experiments, and Maxwell's electromagnetic theory of light address concepts of light's nature (E.1.a) and electromagnetic theory (E.1.b).
Grand Unified Theory covering the unification of electromagnetism with weak and strong forces addresses the relation of electromagnetic radiation to quantum theory (E.2.d).
